title: My Weekend Pottery Class
summary: A learner describes her Saturday afternoon pottery class in Oaxaca, the teacher Doña Rosa, and the slow joy of making her first clay bowl.
body: Every Saturday afternoon, I go to a small pottery studio in the centre of Oaxaca. My teacher is Doña Rosa. She has been making clay pots since she was a child, and her own grandmother taught her. The studio is in the back of an old courtyard with lemon trees and one very lazy cat. There are only six students in the class, which I like, because we can ask questions easily. Last week, I started my first bowl. It was harder than I expected. The clay kept moving under my hands, and twice it collapsed. Doña Rosa just laughed and said that every pot has to fall a few times before it stands. She showed me how to keep my fingers wet and how to use a small wooden tool to smooth the inside. After two hours, I had a simple round bowl. It is not perfect, but I am very proud of it. Next month, I am going to learn how to add colour with natural paints. I have already decided that my first bowl will stay on my kitchen shelf, even if I make better ones later. It taught me that making something slowly is a kind of rest.

annotations.exampleEn: She opened a small pottery studio last year.

annotations.meaningVi: từ khi bà còn là một đứa trẻ
annotations.exampleEn: He has been playing the violin since he was a child.
We had lunch in a quiet old courtyard.
Cooking from a recipe was harder than I expected.
The boat kept moving in the wind.
The tent collapsed when the wind became strong.
The old wall still stands after one hundred years.
Use this cloth to smooth the inside of the bowl.

annotations.phrase: I am very proud of it
annotations.meaningVi: Tôi rất tự hào về nó
annotations.exampleEn: She finished her first painting and is very proud of it.
Children love using natural paints made from plants.
He put the cups on the kitchen shelf.

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect with 'since'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành (have/has + V3) với 'since' để chỉ thời điểm bắt đầu một hành động/tình trạng kéo dài đến hiện tại. Ví dụ: 'She has been making clay pots since she was a child.' — bà bắt đầu từ nhỏ và bây giờ vẫn làm.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use Present Perfect (have/has + past participle) with 'since' to mark the starting point of an action or state that continues now. From the text: 'She has been making clay pots since she was a child.'

grammarNotes.title: 'going to' for future plans
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'be going to + V' diễn tả kế hoạch trong tương lai đã được quyết định trước. Trong bài: 'Next month, I am going to learn how to add colour with natural paints.' Người viết đã có kế hoạch học thêm phần đó.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'be going to + verb' for future plans that are already decided. From the text: 'Next month, I am going to learn how to add colour with natural paints.'

translationVi: Mỗi chiều thứ Bảy, tôi đến một xưởng gốm nhỏ ở trung tâm Oaxaca. Cô giáo của tôi là Doña Rosa. Cô đã nặn nồi đất từ khi còn là một đứa trẻ, và chính bà của cô đã dạy cô. Xưởng nằm phía sau một sân trong cổ, có cây chanh và một chú mèo rất lười. Lớp chỉ có sáu học viên, tôi thích điều đó vì chúng tôi có thể hỏi thoải mái. Tuần trước, tôi bắt đầu chiếc bát đầu tiên của mình. Nó khó hơn tôi nghĩ. Đất sét cứ trượt dưới tay tôi, và đã đổ sụp hai lần. Doña Rosa chỉ cười và nói mọi chiếc nồi đều phải đổ vài lần trước khi đứng vững. Cô chỉ tôi cách giữ ngón tay ướt và cách dùng dụng cụ gỗ nhỏ để làm nhẵn mặt trong. Sau hai giờ, tôi đã có một chiếc bát tròn đơn giản. Nó không hoàn hảo, nhưng tôi rất tự hào về nó. Tháng tới, tôi sẽ học cách tô màu bằng sơn tự nhiên. Tôi đã quyết định chiếc bát đầu tiên sẽ ở mãi trên kệ bếp của mình, dù sau này tôi có làm chiếc đẹp hơn. Nó đã dạy tôi rằng làm gì đó từ tốn là một kiểu nghỉ ngơi.

questions.prompt: When does the narrator go to the pottery class?
questions.explanation: The first sentence says 'Every Saturday afternoon, I go to a small pottery studio.'
questions.hint: Listen for the day and time of day at the very start.

questions.prompt: Who taught Doña Rosa to make pots?

options: At the top of a tall building
In the back of an old courtyard
questions.explanation: 'The studio is in the back of an old courtyard with lemon trees and one very lazy cat.'

questions.prompt: How many students are in the class?
questions.explanation: 'There are only six students in the class.'

questions.prompt: Why does the narrator like the small class size?
options: Because they can ask questions easily
questions.hint: Listen for what is easier in a small class.

questions.prompt: What happened twice during her first bowl-making?
questions.hint: Listen for what happened to the clay shape.

questions.prompt: What did Doña Rosa say about failed pots?
options: Every pot has to fall a few times before it stands
questions.explanation: 'every pot has to fall a few times before it stands.'
questions.hint: Listen for the teacher's wise comment.

questions.prompt: What tool did Doña Rosa show her how to use?
questions.explanation: 'how to use a small wooden tool to smooth the inside.'
questions.hint: Listen for the material of the tool.

questions.prompt: What will the narrator learn next month?
options: How to fire the bowls in an oven
How to sell the bowls in a market
How to add colour with natural paints
questions.explanation: 'I am going to learn how to add colour with natural paints.'

questions.prompt: What does the bowl mean to her personally?
options: Nothing special — she might throw it away
It is a memory of her first try and she wants to keep it
It is a gift for her teacher
It is a copy of her grandmother's bowl
questions.explanation: She decides her first bowl 'will stay on my kitchen shelf, even if I make better ones later.'
questions.hint: Listen for what she decides about the first bowl.

questions.prompt: What is the main lesson the narrator takes from the class?
options: Making something slowly is a kind of rest
questions.explanation: 'It taught me that making something slowly is a kind of rest.'

cloze.template: Every Saturday afternoon, I go to a small pottery studio in the centre of Oaxaca. My teacher is Doña Rosa. She has been making clay pots {{b1}} she was a child, and her own grandmother taught her. The studio is in the back of an old {{b2}} with lemon trees and one very lazy cat. There are only six students in the class, which I like, because we can ask questions easily. Last week, I started my first bowl. It was {{b3}} than I expected. The clay kept moving under my hands, and twice it {{b4}}. Doña Rosa just laughed and said that every pot has to fall a few times before it {{b5}}. She showed me how to keep my fingers wet and how to use a small wooden {{b6}} to smooth the inside. After two hours, I had a simple round bowl. It is not perfect, but I am very {{b7}} of it. Next month, I am going to learn how to add colour with {{b8}} paints. I have already decided that my first bowl will stay on my kitchen {{b9}}, even if I make better ones later. It taught me that making something slowly is a kind of {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: 'since' — used with a point in time (since she was a child).
'courtyard' — an open area inside a building.
'collapsed' — the bowl fell in on itself.
'stands' — a pot 'stands' when it holds its shape.
'tool' — a small wooden tool to smooth the inside.
'proud' — happy and satisfied with what she made.
'natural' — natural paints come from plants and minerals.
'shelf' — the bowl will sit on her kitchen shelf.
'rest' — slow making is a kind of rest, the main lesson.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator says that 'making something slowly is a kind of rest'. Do you agree that slow, hands-on work can feel restful? What is a small, slow activity that calms you, and why do you think it has that effect?
